title: Against Institutional Racism
author: Matthew

opening_move.description: Opening by diagnosing that contemporary discussions of racism have shifted to 'institutional' and 'structural' racism, Matthew immediately states his counter-thesis: much so-called institutional racism is not racism at all, and this extra-individual focus is a mistake that obscures the continuing importance of individual racism. The paper signals a polemical, revisionary stance from the first paragraph.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Contemporary discussions of race should return to a significant focus on individuals; institutional racism as typically theorized (especially extrinsic/disparate impact versions) lacks a plausible account of what makes institutions racist; Sally Haslanger's structural approach faces serious objections.

rivals_engaged: extrinsic institutional racism / disparate impact conception
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper distinguishes intrinsic institutional racism (endorsed) from extrinsic/disparate impact institutional racism (rejected). It accepts that García's affective individual model can explain intrinsic institutional racism. It then systematically critiques extrinsic institutional racism: (1) no plausible account of what makes such institutions racist; (2) overstated explanatory indispensability — individual racism is at least as important; (3) Haslanger's structural account fails to ground the relevant normative claims; (4) institutional reform may be insufficient even if accepted. The paper defends a return to individual-level analysis.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Analytical philosophy of race: precise conceptual distinctions (intrinsic vs extrinsic institutional racism), scenario analysis (S1–S4 cases), immanent critique of rival accounts through internal objections.
epistemic_mode.notes: Classic analytic political philosophy mode: argument proceeds through conceptual distinctions, hypothetical scenarios, and internal critique. No empirical evidence; occasional reference to real-world cases (police killings of Black people) as motivating context, not evidential basis. This is a polemical reconstruction arguing for a minority position against the dominant framing of racism discourse.

normative_stake.domain: philosophy of race / racism / political philosophy

confidence_notes: A distinctive paper in the PSC corpus: it argues against the dominant critical race theory framing, defending an individualist and analytic account. This is a Type 2a variant (polemical reconstruction with strong thesis) but unusual in its direction — most PSC papers argue for more structural, critical, or decolonial positions. Matthew argues for less structural analysis. The move_sequence follows a tight analytic pattern: conceptual_distinction → rival_mapping → immanent_critique → counter_objection → conclusion. No case; no empirical evidence. Classified fully_normative. High confidence in classification.
